purpose: Determines corporate earnings sustainability by decomposing aggregate profits into macro accounting components (Investment + Government Deficit + Net Exports − Household Saving + Dividends).
components: Government fiscal deficit (~$1.8-2.0T, primary driver this cycle)
Net corporate investment
Current account balance (deficit 3-3.5% GDP)
Household saving rate
Dividend payments
current_assessment: Fiscal deficit at 6-7% GDP is the dominant earnings support. Fiscal consolidation >2pp GDP would mechanically reduce aggregate profits, triggering credit cycle acceleration within 4-6 quarters. This framework was judged the correct lens for this cycle over Solow/operating-leverage alternatives.
limitations: Behavioral pass-through elasticities are imprecisely estimated
The 2pp consolidation threshold is a heuristic, not precisely calibrated
Does not specify the timing or composition of fiscal withdrawal
The magnitude of profits above counterfactual (e.g. 30-40% above) is unsupported by rigorous counterfactual specification

purpose: Assesses structural resilience of the expansion by tracking the private sector financial balance (saving minus investment) using the accounting identity that all sectoral balances sum to zero.
components: Government fiscal balance (currently deficit 6-7% GDP)
Private sector financial balance (currently surplus 2-3% GDP)
External balance / current account (currently deficit 3-3.5% GDP)
Historical comparison to prior cycle peaks
current_assessment: Private sector surplus of 2-3% GDP indicates structurally greater resilience than 1999-2000 (deficit ~5%) or 2005-2007 (deficit ~3-4%). The Godley framework correctly predicted both 2001 and 2008 from private deficit readings. Rated as the single most valuable analytical contribution across all agents.
limitations: Only one agent developed this explicitly despite the accounting being verifiable
Does not reveal distribution of surplus across household income deciles
The surplus mechanically depends on the fiscal deficit — not independent
Behavioral responses to surplus erosion are uncertain

purpose: Provides a testable credit cycle timing tool by linking fiscal consolidation (via Kalecki) to Minsky instability thresholds, predicting that fiscal withdrawal triggers credit cycle downshift within 4-6 quarters.
components: Kalecki profit channel (fiscal deficit → corporate profits)
Minsky financing fragility classification (hedge/speculative/Ponzi)
Interest coverage ratio deterioration threshold
Credit cycle acceleration trigger (fiscal consolidation >2pp GDP)
Maturity wall refinancing calendar ($900B-$1.1T, 2025-2027)
current_assessment: The most falsifiable prediction across all agents: fiscal consolidation >2pp GDP should trigger credit cycle downshift within 4-6 quarters. Currently untriggered as fiscal remains expansionary. The maturity wall concentrates refinancing risk in the same window as potential fiscal consolidation events.
limitations: The 4-6 quarter lag is a rough estimate, not precisely calibrated
Fiscal trajectory timing is inherently unpredictable (political)
Private credit opacity may delay or obscure the credit cycle signal
The framework assumes the credit cycle is the binding constraint, which may not hold if fiscal remains dominant

purpose: Determines whether yield curve slope reflects growth expectations or fiscal supply/risk premia, with fundamentally different investment implications for each.
components: ACM term premium model decomposition
Kim-Wright term premium model decomposition
Rate expectations component (forward rates minus term premium)
Good steepening diagnostic (productivity-driven, compresses term premium)
Bad steepening diagnostic (fiscal-supply-driven, expands term premium)
Sahm Rule validation via curve decomposition
current_assessment: Current positive slope is primarily a term premium steepener, not an expectations steepener — superficially resembles mid-cycle but has fundamentally different growth implications. The Sahm Rule false positive in July 2024 is diagnosed via the absence of bull-steepening and stable term premium, validating the methodology.
limitations: ACM and Kim-Wright models diverge on precise decomposition
The decomposition is inherently model-dependent
Practical positioning implications require further development
Does not directly translate to GDP growth forecasts

purpose: Decomposes GDP growth into labor quantity, labor quality, capital deepening, and total factor productivity to assess potential GDP and the sustainability of current growth rates.
components: Labor force growth (including immigration contribution of 0.2-0.5pp)
Labor quality / human capital
Capital deepening
Total factor productivity (~1.5-2% aggregate, no AI structural break)
Explicit component ranges for transparency
current_assessment: Immigration has contributed 0.2-0.5pp annually to potential GDP growth via extensive margin. AI has not yet produced a measurable TFP structural break at the aggregate level. The forward trajectory depends on immigration policy persistence and AI diffusion timeline — both highly uncertain.
limitations: Cannot distinguish between cyclical TFP variation and structural shifts in real time
Immigration contribution is politically contingent and may reverse
Does not incorporate hysteresis effects (demand shaping supply)
Potential GDP estimates are subject to the same 1.0-2.0pp revision problem as the output gap

purpose: Assesses true credit cycle positioning by adjusting standard public indicators for known distortions from covenant-lite structures, EBITDA addbacks, and private credit opacity.
components: Public HY OAS (currently 350-420bp, 40th-55th percentile)
Distressed ratio (currently <5%)
Upgrade/downgrade ratio (currently ~0.8x)
Covenant-lite default delay adjustment (+12-24 months lag)
EBITDA addback adjustment (true leverage 15-40% higher than reported)
Private credit opacity adjustment ($1.7T invisible to public indices)
Effective stall speed (1.5-2.0% GDP vs standard 0-0.5%)
current_assessment: Public indicators suggest mid-cycle, but after adjusting for three systematic distortions, the credit cycle may be further advanced than visible. The economy may be much closer to a credit stress trigger than standard indicators suggest. Any default-rate-based model is structurally lagged.
limitations: Magnitude of private credit optimism bias is unmeasured (could be 5% or 25%)
The 12-24 month cov-lite delay is a rough heuristic from limited sample
Adjustment factors are additive heuristics, not rigorously calibrated
No backtesting of the adjusted framework against prior cycles
